items.title: Decision trees as base learners
items.description: Each forest tree is a decision tree grown on a bootstrap sample; split criteria, impurity, and pruning concepts are defined in the decision trees topic—here we focus on bagging and feature subsampling.

items.title: Variance reduction through averaging
items.description: Random forests target high-variance deep trees by averaging decorrelated predictions; the bias–variance framing explains why bagging stabilizes outputs without a sequential correction loop.

items.title: Contrast with sequential boosting
items.description: Gradient boosting trains shallow trees sequentially on pseudo-residuals with a learning rate; random forests train independent trees in parallel and aggregate votes—a different ensemble strategy.

items.title: Tuning with cross-validation
items.description: Cross-validation selects n_estimators, max_features, and depth by comparing held-out fold scores; forests rarely overfit from more trees alone, but depth and feature fraction still need validation.
